questions.question: Define a binary relation on a set. Give an example of a relation on the set {1,2} that is reflexive but not symmetric.
keyPoints: A binary relation R on a set A is any subset of the Cartesian product A×A.
Example: R = {(1,1),(1,2),(2,2)} is reflexive because (1,1) and (2,2) are present, but not symmetric because (1,2)∈R while (2,1)∉R.

questions.question: Explain what is meant by an equivalence relation. Provide an example of an equivalence relation on the set {1,2,3,4}.
keyPoints: An equivalence relation on a set is a relation that is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
Example: R = {(a,b) | a ≡ b (mod 2)} partitions the set into even and odd elements.

questions.question: Consider the relation R on the set of integers Z defined by aRb if a−b is even. Determine whether R is (i) reflexive, (ii) symmetric, (iii) transitive, (iv) antisymmetric. Hence state whether R is an equivalence relation. Justify your answers.
keyPoints: Symmetric: if a−b is even then b−a = -(a−b) is also even ⇒ (b,a)∈R.
Transitive: if a−b and b−c are even, then a−c = (a−b)+(b−c) is even ⇒ (a,c)∈R.
Not antisymmetric: a≠b but both (a,b) and (b,a) hold (e.g., 1R3 and 3R1). Since R is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive, it is an equivalence relation.

questions.question: Define a partial order. Show that the relation ‘a divides b’ (denoted a|b) on the set of positive integers N is a partial order. Is it a total order? Explain.
keyPoints: A partial order is a relation that is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive.
Reflexive: a|a for every a∈N (a = a·1).
Antisymmetric: if a|b and b|a then a=b (since positive integers).
Transitive: if a|b and b|c then a|c (a = b·k, b = c·m ⇒ a = c·(km)).
Not total: for some a,b neither a|b nor b|a holds (e.g., 2 and 3), hence the relation is a partial order but not a total order.
